coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of methylated carbon quantum dot-based fluorescent films for highly sensitive and stable temperature probes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jiannan
Yan, Ke
Zhang, Pan
Pan, Aizhao
Xiong, Wei
Chen, Xuehang
Zhao, Chunyu
Hong, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Due to their distinct physicochemical characteristics and outstanding biocompatibility, carbon quantum dots have shown to offer enormous potential in the field of temperature sensing. However, there is still much to learn about the mechanism underlying carbon quantum dots' thermal sensing capabilities, and developing incredibly precise thermometers based on it is still an enormous obstacle. Herein, we successfully fabricated carbon quantum dots with variable luminescence wavelength and temperature-sensitive properties by adjusting the ingredients to modify the composition of functional groups (-NH2, -OH, -COOH and -CH2OH) on the surface of quantum dots in a simple hydrothermal synthesis process, followed by encapsulation with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). Subsequently, structural/optical characterisation and temperature-dependent studies were utilized for investigations into the quantum dot fluorescence mechanisms as well as the thermal sensing characteristics connected to surface functional groups, respectively. Notably, the designed red-emitting methylenated carbon quantum dot film probe displayed more linear segmented temperature sensing properties (R2>0.99), a wider temperature applicability range (20–160 °C), and high temperature reversibility (20–80 °C, at least 4 cycles). Finally, the mechanism by which functional groups affect the thermal characteristics of carbon quantum dots was addressed, and we hypothesized that intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions are important factors affecting the thermal properties of carbon dots. This work presents great promise and theoretical guidance for the development of highly reliable carbon quantum dot temperature sensing systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Temperature response
Functional groups
Probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence and micromorphology images of carbon quantum dots. (a) Photoluminescence image of carbon quantum dots in aqueous solution. (b-d) Typical TEM images of blue, green, and red carbon quantum dots. (e-g) Size distribution of blue, green, and red carbon quantum dots.

(a) XRD patterns of CQDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CQDs. (c) Photoluminescence spectroscopy of blue carbon quantum dots. (d) Photoluminescence spectroscopy of green carbon quantum dots. (e) Photoluminescence spectroscopy of red carbon quantum dots.

Optical properties of the synthesized CQD/PVA films (a) Fabricated CQD/PVA films and their luminescence influenced by functional groups schematically. (b) Photoluminescence and UV absorption spectra of blue CQD/PVA film. (c) Photoluminescence and UV absorption spectra of green CQD/PVA film. (d) Photoluminescence and UV absorption spectra of blue CQD/PVA film.

Temperature response characteristics of CQD/PVA films (a) PL spectral variation of the heating process of the blue CQD/PVA film temperature probe. (b) PL spectral variation of the heating process of the green CQD/PVA film temperature probe. (c) PL spectral variation of the heating process of the red CQD/PVA film temperature probe. (d) Temperature dependence of the red CQD/PVA film temperature probe. (e) The temperature-dependent reversibility of the red CQD/PVA film temperature probe. (f) Fluorescence images of red CQD/PVA film at different temperatures. (g) Optical and temperature response characteristics influenced by functional groups.
The schematic diagram for the preparation of carbon QD/PVA films.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of methylated carbon quantum dot-based fluorescent films for highly sensitive and stable temperature probes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their distinct physicochemical characteristics and outstanding biocompatibility, carbon quantum dots have shown to offer enormous potential in the field of temperature sensing. However, there is still much to learn about the mechanism underlying carbon quantum dots' thermal sensing capabilities, and developing incredibly precise thermometers based on it is still an enormous obstacle. Herein, we successfully fabricated carbon quantum dots with variable luminescence wavelength and temperature-sensitive properties by adjusting the ingredients to modify the composition of functional groups (-NH2, -OH, -COOH and -CH2OH) on the surface of quantum dots in a simple hydrothermal synthesis process, followed by encapsulation with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). Subsequently, structural/optical characterisation and temperature-dependent studies were utilized for investigations into the quantum dot fluorescence mechanisms as well as the thermal sensing characteristics connected to surface functional groups, respectively. Notably, the designed red-emitting methylenated carbon quantum dot film probe displayed more linear segmented temperature sensing properties (R2>0.99), a wider temperature applicability range (20–160 °C), and high temperature reversibility (20–80 °C, at least 4 cycles). Finally, the mechanism by which functional groups affect the thermal characteristics of carbon quantum dots was addressed, and we hypothesized that intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions are important factors affecting the thermal properties of carbon dots. This work presents great promise and theoretical guidance for the development of highly reliable carbon quantum dot temperature sensing systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature is considered as a crucial indicator in the realm of measurement as it plays a key role in understanding physical, chemical, and biological processes [1–5]. To date, various measurement approaches have been developed to obtain the temperature information of the target [6–10]. Among them, nanomaterials with optical sensing capabilities have been of particular interest to researchers owing to their tunable spectral characteristics and non-contact measurement characteristics [11–16]. Numerous types of fluorescent nanomaterials (e.g., fluorescent dyes, quantum dots, inorganic phosphors, and metal-organic frameworks) have yielded some advances in thermometry applications. For example, recent reports [17,18] have shown that quantum dot materials, represented by chalcogenide, CdTe, etc., are gradually becoming an emerging temperature sensing material, which can be made an excellent platform for temperature monitoring by means of surface modification [19,20], customization of heterostructures [21], and so on. Zhao et al. [22] have revealed that metal lanthanide elemental organic frameworks serve as a potential material for self-calibrating luminescent sensors that could quickly and easily identify temperature and antibiotics, among other things. However, some drawbacks remain to be urgently addressed in the field of temperature measurement technology, such as the toxicity and potential environmental hazards associated with some temperature-sensitive materials, which may impose limitations on measurement applications. A related study [21] also pointed out that the sensitivity of quantum dots to pH or other biomolecules hinders the commercialization of these quantum dot-based thermal sensors, and that Cd- or Pb-based quantum dots need to be replaced by quantum dots with non-toxic elements or lower toxicity. In the field of temperature sensing, the design of optical nano-temperature probes that take advantage of materials' photothermal sensing properties has recently become a popular research topic. As a result, the development of nano-temperature gauge materials with excellent optical properties that are safe for use on people and the environment has also gained attention.

In contemporary times, the popularity of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has risen notably due to their robust luminescence, remarkable chemical stability, insignificant toxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility [23–27]. Carbon quantum dot-based fluorescent materials with tailored properties have become a hot research topic for probe materials [28–30]. Recent reports on carbon dots and their hybrids have demonstrated their feasibility for application as luminescence-based nanothermometers. Liu et al. [31] found that water-soluble carbon nanodots can be applied to the detection of a variety of physical or chemical stimuli such as temperature, pH, and Fe3+, while the mechanism of the C dot sensing capacities and the C dot intracellular sensing behaviors still need to be further investigated. Tang et al. [32] demonstrated that CQDs exhibit temperature behavior similar to that of metal nanoclusters and speculated that the π-electrons in CQDs could act like free electrons in metal nanoclusters, which revealed the thermal dependence of the luminescence of carbon quantum dots to a certain extent, but lacked an in-depth analysis of the thermal sensing ability of carbon quantum dots. Recently, in a study on cellular heat sensing, Li and colleagues [33] evaluated the biocompatibility of C quantum dots by performing a assay on the cell line to test cell viability at different concentrations of C quantum dots and demonstrated that there was no significant decrease in cell viability as the incubation time was increased from 24 h to 120 h when the concentration of C dots was lower than 75 μg ml hr−1, suggesting that C quantum dots are very promising candidates for nanoscale temperature measurements in vivo in humans. Moreover, in further research investigations of carbon quantum dots, functional groups are considered to be an integral part of the carbon dot synthesis process and play a crucial role in the characterization of the quantum dots. It has been indicated [34] that a wide range of modulation of carbon dot solutions can be achieved via varying the number of functional groups on the surface of the synthesized functionalized carbon dots. Recent studies have pointed out that the surface state of carbon dots is the key to the luminescence of C-dots [35], for example, researchers have observed the red-shifted emission of C-dots with high surface oxidation [36]. And It has also been proved in various studies that various functional groups on the surface of carbon dots can also modulate the sensing ability of carbon dots [35,37–39]. The functionalization and application of carbon quantum dots has become a hot topic in carbon quantum dot research. However, as can be noticed from the above studies, little research has been conducted on the luminescence mechanism of the surface functional groups on the carbon quantum dots, especially the temperature-sensitive properties.

The controlled modulation of the surface functional groups of quantum dots could be employed to develop temperature chemical sensors with multiple characteristics, which renders the introduction of surface functional groups for CQDs of substantial research interest. Herein, the CQD/PVA films with tunable photoluminescence colors (blue, green and red, functional groups related) were fabricated and the temperature dependence of the films was tested. On this basis, the mechanism of the influence of surface functional groups on the luminescence of quantum dots was further investigated. The temperature test results revealed that the CQD/PVA films displayed perfect temperature measurement ability based on PL intensity, with temperature dependence related to the surface functional groups of the quantum dots. Among them, the methylenated CQD/PVA temperature probe with red emission characteristics exhibited optimal segmented linear temperature dependence based on fluorescence intensity, indicating the potential of functional group-controlled carbon quantum dots for temperature sensing. The temperature-dependent luminescence sensing mechanism associated with carbon quantum dot functional groups was further discussed from the perspective of the influence of surface functional groups. The above work demonstrated the considerable potential of carbon quantum dots as temperature sensing probes.

All starting materials were obtained from commercial sources and were ready to use without further purification. P-phenylenediamine (PDA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Meryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The blue-emitted carbon quantum dots in this study were synthesized according to the simple method developed by Qu et al. [40]. Namely, they were obtained by mixing a certain proportion of CA and DETA in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, followed by a reaction at 180 °C for 6 hours. The prepared carbon quantum dot solution was dried into a powder, ethanol was added to configure the solution, and stored in the refrigerator for backup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of green and red carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: For green carbon quantum dots, 50 mg of p-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 60 ml of deionized water under stirring until dissolution. The solution was then added to a sealed Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the autoclave was put into a constant temperature drying oven, followed by a reaction at 180 °C for 12 hours. Under the same synthetic procedure, the red carbon quantum dots were obtained by adding 50 mg of p-phenylenediamine and 50 mg of citric acid were dissolved in 60 ml of deionized water. The prepared carbon quantum dot solution was dried into a powder, added to ethanol to configure the solution and kept in a refrigerator for backup.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with a mass fraction of 4 % was added to a solution of carbon quantum dots possessing diverse functional groups, followed by heating and stirring until dissolution for subsequent fabrication of temperature-sensitive CQD/PVA films. The solution was dropped and uniformly distributed on the surface of the glass plate, generating a uniform CQD/PVA sensing film after the evaporation of the solvent. The dimensions of the film can be adjusted to meet the actual requirements of temperature measurement.

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM), an FEI G2F30 electron microscope with a Gatan SC 200 CCD camera equipped with an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was employed. For material crystal structure characterization, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using a Bruker AXS D8 Discover X-ray diffractometer at a wavelength of Cu K (1.79). A Bruker VERTEX70 micro-infrared spectrometer was used to obtain infrared spectra of carbon quantum dots. An Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 spectrophotometer with an integrated nanowire was used to record fluorescence spectra. A laser, a spectrometer, an optical fiber, a digital thermostat, an MX 100 data gathering device, and a platinum resistance temperature sensor are all parts of the temperature dependency experimental platform.

The carbon quantum dot solutions prepared by the hydrothermal route are illustrated in Fig. 1a, which displays bright luminescence with different colors under UV excitation light. Typical TEM images of carbon quantum dots emitting various luminescence are depicted in Fig. 1b–d. As observed in the figure, the carbon quantum dots exhibited irregular particle shapes. The high-resolution TEM images of the single carbon quantum dot are displayed in the upper right panel of Fig. 1b–d, from which the lattice stripes of the quantum dot are observed, demonstrating the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 1e–f illustrate the statistics of the size distribution of the carbon quantum dots, from which it can be observed that the dimensions of the quantum dots were distributed in a normal curve, and there was no significant discrepancy between the average sizes of the synthesized blue, green, and red quantum dots (2.91 nm, 3.01 nm, and 3.07 nm, respectively), indicating no significant relationship between the fluorescence color and the size of the carbon quantum dots.

The crystalline phase of the Carbon quantum dots was revealed by XRD, as shown in Fig. 2a. The major characteristic peak appearing at 2θ = 19.6° was confirmed for all three carbon quantum dots, corresponding to the (0 0 2) carbon plane, attributable to the highly disordered carbon [41]. Furthermore, FT-IR spectra of quantum dots with varying colors were displayed to investigate the surface structure and functional groups of the prepared quantum dots, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. For blue quantum dots, the absorption band around 3431 cm−1 corresponded to the presence of amino groups (-NH2) on the surface of the blue quantum dots, the absorption band at 1695 cm−1 was assigned to the carbonyl group (-CO) stretching vibration. For the green quantum dots, the strong absorption band around 3343 cm−1 indicated that the red quantum dots possessed amino groups (-NH2) on the surface. For the red quantum dots, the strong absorption band around 3213 cm−1 was associated with the presence of amino (-NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups in the CQD. The absorption bands at 2920 and 2850 cm−1 were related to the presence of the -CH2 group, and the strong absorption band at 1714 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the carbonyl group (-CO) stretching vibration. These functional group indications suggested the existence of functional groups of amino, carboxyl, carbonyl, and methylene groups on the surface of the red quantum dots. Fig. 2c–e reveal the photoluminescence spectra of carbon quantum dot solutions. The quantum dot solutions exhibited various fluorescence wavelengths and luminescence colors depending on the surface functional groups of the carbon quantum dots, which further proved the modulating effect of functional groups on the luminescent properties of carbon quantum dots.

Fig. 3a displays the schematic photoluminescence of CQD/PVA films with diverse surface functional groups. The photoluminescence color of CQD/PVA films gradually evolved from blue to red with the variation of the composition of functional groups on the surface of quantum dots under UV irradiation, which was presumably caused by the change of energy state of carbon quantum dots under the action of distinct surface functional groups.

The photoluminescence patterns and UV absorption spectra of CQD/PVA films encapsulating different surface functional groups are depicted in Fig. 3b–d. A red shift in the peak wavelength of the quantum dots was observed according to the change of the functional groups on the surface of the quantum dots. As illustrated in Fig. 3b–d, the fabricated CQD/PVA films displayed a typical absorption range of 400–700 nm, with a clear red shift in the intense absorption starting from the CQD at the -OH, -COOH and -NH2 (420 nm) to -NH2 at 500 nm and then to -OH, -COOH, -NH2 and -CH2OH (680 nm). Furthermore, the luminescence mechanism of functional groups affecting quantum dots was deduced for the variation of the photoluminescence emission wavelength of quantum dot films: Based on the green carbon quantum dots with -NH2 group, the energy stability of CQDs is improved by introducing -OH and -COOH groups on the surface of quantum dots through the addition of citric acid, leading to the blue shift of wavelength. Additionally, the introduction of the methylene-related group (-CH2OH) resulted in an enhanced relaxation mechanism, leading to the wavelength red-shift of the quantum dots and the red characteristic emission of the fluorescent film. This further illustrated the mechanism by which the surface functional groups affect the luminescence of carbon quantum dots.

There is considerable potential to use carbon quantum dots as a unique optical sensor by monitoring their fluorescent intensity changes in response to external physical or chemical stimuli. Herein, the temperature responsiveness of the CQD/PVA film probes was investigated on the constructed temperature test platform. Fig. 4a–c demonstrate the evolution of PL spectra (405 nm excitation) of CQD/PVA films with diverse functional groups as the temperature was increased. The fluorescence intensity of CQD/PVA films all exhibited a decreasing trend with increasing temperature, while no significant change in the peak fluorescence wavelength of the films was observed, indicating a very small temperature dependence of PL peak wavelength for all three prepared films, which was in line with the previous study [42–44]. In addition, the studies have revealed that it is challenging to measure the temperature at the nanoscale based on the offset of the PL peak [10]. In contrast, as observed in Fig. 4c, the red quantum dot film manifested a more prominent and consistent decreasing trend in terms of peak intensity variation in the 20–160 °C range, indicating its high potential and wide thermal response range for temperature sensing applications.

The temperature dependence of fluorescence intensity was examined separately for CQD/PVA films (Fig. 4 and Fig. S-1, supporting information). Among them, Fig. 4b illustrates the temperature-dependent behavior of the red film probes, whereby the films exhibited a superior segmented linear variation relationship with increasing temperature in the range of 20–160 °C, where the inflection point of the temperature-dependent curve was approximately 95 °C. Furthermore, Pearson's r (Pearson's correlation coefficient) was introduced to evaluate the thermal performance of the carbon quantum dot film, and a better fit was judged when its value was close to 1: For the two temperature-dependent curves in the low and high temperature intervals, the linear coefficient of the red CQD/PVA film probe was 0.99328 and 0.99661, respectively (S-2, supporting information), signalling an excellent thermal response potential. However, we investigated the temperature-dependent characteristics of blue and green CQD/PVA films (Fig. S-1, Supporting Information), and the comparison revealed that the red film probe possessed a wider temperature response range and a more excellent linear fit than the other two.

The fluorescent intensity reversibility of the red CQD/PVA film probe with temperature is provided in Fig. 4e to investigate the temperature reversibility index of the film. The process of heating and cooling the film probe was performed at cycling temperatures from 20 to 80 °C to observe the trends in the fluorescence intensity of the film. As observed in the figure, the film probe displayed excellent optical properties during the thermal cycling test from 20 to 80 °C, i.e., the temperature switching operation could be reproduced continuously for at least 4 cycles without major changes, demonstrating its reversibility and reliability as an optical probe for thermometer applications. Besides, Fig. 4f illustrates the photoluminescence of the CQD/PVA film at different temperatures, and it is evident that the film probes remained relatively brightly luminescent below 180 °C, which suggests the excellent thermal stability of the film probe. As a result of its excellent linearity, high sensitivity, and significant reversibility with temperature, the CQD/PVA film probe with red characteristic emission offers tremendous potential for thermal sensing applications.

Finally, the mechanism by which the surface functional groups affect the thermal properties of CQD/PVA film probes was discussed (Fig. 4g). It is well known that the variations in the interfacial interactions between materials tend to affect the thermal stability of the composites. Based on this theory, we hypothesize that the different trends in temperature-dependent changes in the three composite polymer film probes are due to the interaction of intramolecular hydrogen bonding (within the molecules of the luminescent CQDs) as well as intermolecular hydrogen bonding (additional hydrogen bonding between the CQDs and the surface functional groups of the PVA branched chains). In the molding process, the addition of CQDs provides additional hydrogen bonding to form a more stable chain structure with PVA, while intramolecular hydrogen bonds are similarly formed between the functional groups of the quantum dots or PVA. In particular, we hypothesize that for red-emitting carbon quantum dots, the abundant oxygen-containing groups (-OH, -COOH, and -CH2OH) on the surface of the quantum dots connect to the flexible chains of PVA through hydrogen-bonding interactions [45–49], which improves the interfacial compatibility, creates a good thermal transport channel and promotes the heat conduction to enhance the thermal properties of the composite films. When heated at high temperatures, the luminescence intensity of the composite films decreased, which was likely due to the breakage of the hydrogen bond between CQD and PVA by thermal energy [50]. While the blue- and green-emitting CQDs contain more amino groups in their surface functional groups, and the lone pair of electrons from the strong electron-donating group (amino groups) doped onto the aromatic rings of CQDs can be excited to the point that the aromatic rings form a p-π conjugation [51,52], which leads to a greater tendency for the quantum dots to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. This promotes phase separation between the carbon quantum dots and PVA, thereby weakening the thermosensitive properties and thermal stability of the composite films.

In this work, carbon QD/PVA films with different photoluminescent colors were successfully fabricated by controlling the surface functional groups of quantum dots. Subsequently, the mechanism of the effect of the functional groups on the luminescence of carbon quantum dots was analyzed through structural and optical characterization techniques. Temperature-dependent optical experiments were carried out to investigate the temperature responsiveness of the carbon quantum dot films, and the results demonstrated that the prepared CQD/PVA films displayed thermal sensing capability based on PL intensity. Among them, red -emitting CQD/PVA temperature probes with methylene surface functional groups exhibited superior segmented linear temperature dependence, higher temperature sensing range, and thermal reversibility performance than the green and blue CQDs, indicating the temperature sensing potential of carbon quantum dots controlled by functional groups. Finally, the thermal sensing mechanism associated with the functional groups was discussed. In conclusion, this work investigated the role of surface functional groups in modulating the luminescence and temperature-sensitive properties of carbon quantum dots, thereby developing a red-emitting carbon quantum dot temperature probe with a wide temperature sensing range and reversible performance, and the above work provides an effective strategy for designing and tuning highly reliable carbon quantum dot temperature sensing systems.

Pan Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Ke Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wei Xiong: Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Aizhao Pan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jiannan Sun: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Chunyu Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Xuehang Chen: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jun Hong: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Science Center for Gas Turbine Project (NO. P2023-B-I-002-001) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (NO. xzy022024090).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emissive CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC composite for self-calibrating temperature sensing application

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, J. Tian, L. Wang, Y. Zhang, X. Qin, Y. Luo, A.M. Asiri, A.O. Al-Youbi, X. Mater., 24 (2012) 2037-2041.
